facade_colour: warm red-brown brick with dark brown/black window frames and turquoise-painted ground floor door

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including mirrored facades, cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay windows, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, decorative wooden gable brackets and bargeboards, and fenestration pattern with mix of segmental and flat arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: warm red-brown brick with dark brown/black window frames and turquoise-painted ground floor door
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Brick facade intact at upper levels; ground floor has colourful painted door and altered entry but structural form preserved. Some graffiti/street art on neighbouring walls.
photo_observations.window_details: Two windows per floor on ground and second storey within the bay window projection; single narrow window in the gable peak. Dark-framed replacement windows throughout.
photo_observations.door_details: Turquoise-painted single door at ground level beside the bay window, with colourful painted surround and small awning.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Decorative bargeboard and trim in the gable peak; bracketed eave details visible.
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Three-sided canted bay window spanning ground and second floor on the right side of the facade, consistent with Bay-and-Gable typology.
photo_observations.porch_details: Low concrete stoop with 2-3 steps at the entry beside the bay window.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Decorative brickwork visible at upper levels; bargeboard trim in the gable; brick voussoirs/lintels over windows.
photo_observations.signage_observed: No commercial signage; colourful painted door surround with street art/mural elements at ground level.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Replacement windows throughout; ground floor entry repainted in bright colours but original bay-and-gable massing fully intact.
photo_observations.notes: Clear facade photo shows intact Bay-and-Gable semi-detached form. Double-height bay window and steep front gable are character-defining features. Ground floor colourfully altered but original form legible.

notes: Based on a limited adjacent streetscape view in the Nassau run.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include bay window, original windows, decorative brick, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including mirrored facades, cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay windows, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, decorative wooden gable brackets and bargeboards, and fenestration pattern with mix of segmental and flat arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Semi-detached bay-and-gable house (1890-1903) in warm red-brown brick with cream trim and decorative brickwork. Three storeys under a cross-gable, with a three-sided canted bay spanning the lower two floors and a single gable-peak window above. Turquoise-painted ground-floor door and segmental arch at entry. Constructed as a mirrored pair with 60 Nassau St. Subdivided into 3 dwelling units. Party walls on both sides.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including mirrored facades, cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay windows, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, decorative wooden gable brackets and bargeboards, and fenestration pattern with mix of segmental and flat arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
